summary: Matt Rocklin explores how positions of leverage—where small efforts yield outsized benefits for others—can paradoxically lead to burnout and self-sacrifice. He argues that the obligation to help others, combined with the good feelings it produces, causes people to suppress natural release mechanisms like rest and emotional expression. When this leads to a breaking point, the solution isn't simply working less, but creatively redesigning one's relationship to work around personal needs. Drawing from his experience nearly quitting as a startup founder multiple times, he shows how selfish actions like eliminating meetings and logging off Slack actually improved outcomes for everyone.
key_points: Positions of leverage create a moral trap: when your small sacrifice saves others significant time, refusing to help feels monstrous
Natural release mechanisms (venting, taking time off) stop making sense when you're in a position of leverage, causing identity erosion
The breaking point is paradoxically when egotism and altruism align—fixing yourself becomes the most important project
Creative selfishness (no recurring meetings, disconnecting from Slack, extended breaks) is more effective than simply 'working less'
High-leverage positions actually grant more power to reshape work around personal needs, not less
money_quotes: Sacrificing minutes of your time saves days of someone else's, so of course you sacrifice. You'd be a monster not to.
Your leverage swallows part of your identity and, eventually, you break.
You can't fix yourself through more sacrifice. You can only fix things by selfishly prioritizing your needs.
This is a rare moment in life where egotism and altruism are perfectly aligned.
It's either that or you and the whole system break down.
No one has full freedom over their worklife, but everyone has a lot of freedom, far more than they exercise.
Be selfish, creatively. Don't just stop doing work or helping others; figure out very specifically what you want and design work around what you want.
key_insight: When you're in a high-leverage position, creative selfishness isn't just acceptable—it's necessary for sustainability, and the higher your leverage, the more power you have to reshape work around your needs.
